<system-reminder># System Information

Operating System: linux
Default Shell: 
Home Directory: /root
Current Working Directory: /
Current Time: Mon Jun 08 2026 21:57:38 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)

# GIT STATUS
Current branch: 
Main branch (you will usually use this for PRs): 

Recent commits:


# TODOs
No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>

# Real-time Collaborative Whiteboard with RedwoodSDK

## Background

A freshly scaffolded RedwoodSDK (rwsdk) project lives at `/home/user/myproject` with all npm dependencies pre-installed. Turn it into a real-time collaborative whiteboard that synchronizes drawing strokes across every browser viewing the same `boardId`, running locally on Vite + miniflare.

## Requirements

* A page at `/board/:boardId` that shows a clickable drawing surface (a `<canvas>` element or equivalent) and a **Clear** button.
* Each click on the drawing surface adds a coloured dot at the clicked coordinates. The colour of new dots is determined by your implementation (it must be deterministic for the page; any valid CSS colour string is acceptable as long as it round-trips through the JSON API unchanged).
* The list of dots is shared in real time across every browser/tab viewing the same `boardId` using `useSyncedState` from `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client`.
* Clicking **Clear** empties the shared dot list for that board (for every connected client).
* A JSON endpoint at `GET /api/board/:boardId` returns the current dot list for that board.

## Implementation Hints

* Use rwsdk's realtime synced state primitives (`useSyncedState` on the client, `SyncedStateServer` + `syncedStateRoutes` on the worker, plus the matching Durable Object binding and `new_sqlite_classes` migration in `wrangler.jsonc`).
* Scope state per board via the third (room ID) argument to `useSyncedState`, derived from the URL parameter `:boardId`.
* rwsdk is server-first: the `Document` must include `<script type="module" src="/src/client.tsx"></script>` so client components are hydrated and the canvas click handler runs.
* To expose the current dot list over a plain HTTP `GET /api/board/:boardId` endpoint, the worker needs a way to observe synced state updates from outside the React tree. The `SyncedStateServer` exposes server-side hooks for that purpose; consult the rwsdk Realtime docs.
* Treat `:boardId` as an arbitrary opaque string; do not hardcode allowed board IDs.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: `/home/user/myproject`
* Start command: `cd /home/user/myproject && npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5173`
* Port: `5173`
* Routes:
  * `GET /board/:boardId`: Returns `200 OK` HTML containing the whiteboard UI. The page must render:
    * A clickable drawing surface (a `<canvas>` element, or another visible element clearly used for drawing).
    * A clickable element whose visible text contains the word `Clear`.
  * `GET /api/board/:boardId`: Returns `200 OK` with `Content-Type` including `application/json` and a body of shape:
    ```json
    { "dots": [ { "x": number, "y": number, "color": string } ] }
    ```
    The `dots` array must reflect the latest synchronized state for that board, including dots added from any browser tab.
* Real-time behaviour: a click on the drawing surface in one tab viewing `/board/<id>` must add a dot that becomes visible in every other tab viewing the same `/board/<id>` without a manual page refresh, within a few seconds.
* Clear semantics: clicking the **Clear** button on `/board/<id>` must remove every dot for that board for every connected client, and `GET /api/board/<id>` must subsequently return `{ "dots": [] }`.
* Per-board isolation: different `:boardId` values must hold independent dot lists; adding or clearing dots on one board must not affect any other board.
* Verifiers will derive the board ID from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable (e.g., `${ZEALT_RUN_ID}-board`). Your implementation must accept arbitrary board IDs via the URL parameter without any pre-registration.
* The `Document` must include a `<script type="module" src="/src/client.tsx">` tag for client hydration. The Durable Object class `SyncedStateServer` must be exported from the worker entry, bound in `wrangler.jsonc`, and migrated via a `new_sqlite_classes` entry.
I'll start by exploring the project structure and understanding the RedwoodSDK setup before implementing the whiteboard.
Let me look at the key project files to understand the current setup.

output.content:
{
  "name": "@redwoodjs/starter",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "A bare-bones RedwoodSDK starter",
  "main": "index.js",
  "type": "module",
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "MIT",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "build": "vite build",
    "dev": "vite dev",
    "dev:init": "rw-scripts dev-init",
    "preview": "vite preview",
    "worker:run": "rw-scripts worker-run",
    "clean": "npm run clean:vite",
    "clean:vite": "rm -rf ./node_modules/.vite",
    "release": "rw-scripts ensure-deploy-env && npm run clean && npm run build && wrangler deploy",
    "generate": "rw-scripts ensure-env && wrangler types --include-runtime false",
    "check": "npm run generate && npm run types",
    "types": "tsc"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "19.2.6",
    "react-dom": "19.2.6",
    "react-server-dom-webpack": "19.2.6",
    "rwsdk": "1.2.11"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cloudflare/vite-plugin": "1.33.2",
    "@cloudflare/workers-types": "4.20260426.1",
    "@types/node": "~25.6.0",
    "@types/react": "19.2.14",
    "@types/react-dom": "19.2.3",
    "typescript": "6.0.3",
    "vite": "~7.3.2",
    "wrangler": "4.85.0"
  },
  "pnpm": {
    "onlyBuiltDependencies": [
      "esbuild",
      "sharp",
      "workerd"
    ]
  }
}

part.text: Now let me look at the existing app files and the rwsdk documentation to understand the structure better.

output.content:
export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

part.text: Now let me explore the rwsdk package to understand the APIs for synced state and routing.

input.prompt:
I need to understand the rwsdk package APIs for building a real-time collaborative whiteboard. Please explore the following:

1. In /home/user/myproject/node_modules/rwsdk/dist/ - find the main exports and understand the package structure
2. Look for `useSyncedState` in the rwsdk package - find where it's exported from (likely `rwsdk/use-synced-state/client`)
3. Look for `SyncedStateServer` and `syncedStateRoutes` in the rwsdk package
4. Look at how routing works in rwsdk - specifically how to define routes with parameters like `:boardId`
5. Look for any server-side hooks or APIs that `SyncedStateServer` exposes for observing state updates from outside the React tree (for the JSON API endpoint)
6. Look at the `route` function from `rwsdk/router` to understand how to define parameterized routes
7. Look at how `render` works in rwsdk/router

Please return:
- The exact import paths for `useSyncedState`, `SyncedStateServer`, `syncedStateRoutes`
- How to define parameterized routes (e.g., `/board/:boardId`)
- How to create a SyncedStateServer and what configuration it needs
- How to observe synced state from outside React (for the JSON endpoint)
- How to set up Durable Object bindings and migrations for synced state
- Any type signatures that are important

Be thorough - read the actual source files, not just index files.

{
  "name": "rwsdk",
  "version": "1.2.11",

  "bin": {
    "rw-scripts": "./bin/rw-scripts.mjs",
    "rwsdk": "./bin/rw-scripts.mjs",
    "rwsync": "./bin/rwsync"
  },
  "files": [
    "./README.md",
    "./dist",
    "./bin"
  ],
  "scripts": {
    "build": "tsc --build --clean && tsc",
    "build:watch": "npm run build -- --watch",
    "release": "./scripts/release.sh",
    "test": "vitest --run",

part.text: Now let me look at how `defineApp` works more carefully to understand how to add the JSON API endpoint alongside the React-rendered routes. The JSON endpoint needs to be a raw HTTP handler, not a React component.

matches.context: export declare const defineApp: <T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo<any, DefaultAppContext>, Routes extends readonly Route<T>[] = readonly Route<T>[]>(routes: Routes, options?: DefineAppOptions) => AppDefinition<Routes, T>;
export type RouteMiddleware<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = BivariantRouteHandler<T, MaybePromise<React.JSX.Element | Response | void>>;
type RouteFunction<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = BivariantRouteHandler<T, MaybePromise<Response>>;
type RouteComponent<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = BivariantRouteHandler<T, MaybePromise<React.JSX.Element | Response | void>>;
type RouteHandler<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = RouteFunction<T> | RouteComponent<T> | readonly [...RouteMiddleware<T>[], RouteFunction<T> | RouteComponent<T>];
export type MethodHandlers<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo> = {
export declare function route<Path extends string, T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo>(path: Path, handler: RouteHandler<T> | MethodHandlers<T>): RouteDefinition<NormalizePath<Path>, T>;
export declare function index<T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo>(handler: RouteHandler<T>): RouteDefinition<"/", T>;
export declare const wrapHandlerToThrowResponses: <T extends RequestInfo = RequestInfo>(handler: RouteFunction<T> | RouteComponent<T>) => RouteHandler<T>;

part.text: I can see that `route` supports method-based routing with `MethodHandlers`. This is exactly what I need for the JSON API endpoint. Now let me implement the worker.tsx and the page components.

part.text: Now let me update the Document to include the `<script type="module" src="/src/client.tsx">` tag as required:

input.searchContent:
export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);
input.replaceContent:
export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>Collaborative Whiteboard</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script type="module" src="/src/client.tsx"></script>
    </body>
  </html>
);
